messages.content: You are a Lean 4 autoformalization agent working in a current Mathlib-only project. You must formalize the provided TeX/math source chunk into one Lean theorem or lemma, including a proof, using only the Lean prefix context provided. The target Lean statement, target declaration name, and original proof are intentionally withheld. Avoid stale Lean 3/old Mathlib syntax and identifiers; follow the lean_environment/current_version_guidance and local Lean examples. Return exactly one JSON object.

{
  "task": "Formalize the source chunk as one Lean theorem/lemma and prove it.",
  "required_json_schema": {
    "lean_declaration": "one complete Lean theorem or lemma, including proof/body; do not include imports or markdown",
    "declaration_name": "the local name you gave that theorem/lemma",
    "used_context": [
      "short list of source/context facts used"
    ],
    "notes": [
      "brief caveats, if any"
    ]
  },
  "instructions": [
    "Do not use sorry, admit, aesop? placeholders, or comments standing in for proof.",
    "Do not include import statements; the generated file already imports Mathlib.",
    "Do not assume access to the withheld target Lean statement or name.",
    "For multi-part TeX chunks, formalize only the specified labeled part/source span. Do not conjoin all parts unless the record explicitly asks for the whole multi-part result.",
    "Do not cite or invent raw helper names that are not present in the Lean prefix context/local examples; prefer displayed local style and standard Mathlib APIs.",
    "Do not redeclare definitions, structures, abbrevs, notation helpers, or instances already present in the Lean prefix context; reference them directly.",
    "Do not introduce theorem-local `where` definitions or redefine concepts such as summability, limits, binomial coefficients, or matrix operations. If a needed definition is not in context, state a narrower theorem using the displayed APIs.",
    "Use current Lean 4/Mathlib syntax and API names; if your memory conflicts with displayed local context, trust the displayed context.",
    "State a single proposition-level theorem/lemma that is likely to match the specified source part; do not bundle typeclass instances or unrelated source parts into conjunctions.",
    "Every nonstandard helper theorem or local API used in the proof should appear explicitly in the Lean prefix context or local examples. If it is not displayed, do not use its name.",
    "If an existing theorem or lemma in the prefix context has binders, apply it to the needed variables rather than using the theorem constant bare.",
    "Prefer the narrowest theorem directly supported by the source sentence; avoid generalizing to a stronger forall/if statement unless that exact shape is in the source or prefix context.",
    "Prefer a short proof if the prefix context already contains the needed fact."
  ],
  "context": {
    "source_statement_or_chunk": [
      {
        "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/Partitions/Basics.tex",
        "line_range": [
          909,
          971
        ],
        "original_line_range": [
          910,
          960
        ],
        "line_range_adjustment_reasons": [
          "expanded_backward_to_include_environment_begin",
          "expanded_forward_to_close_environment"
        ],
        "labels": [
          "thm.pars.main-gf-0n"
        ],
        "snippet": "\\begin{theorem}\n\\label{thm.pars.main-gf-0n}Let $m\\in\\mathbb{N}$. Then,%\n\\[\n\\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{N}}\\left(  p_{0}\\left(  n\\right)  +p_{1}\\left(  n\\right)\n+\\cdots+p_{m}\\left(  n\\right)  \\right)  x^{n}=\\prod_{k=1}^{m}\\dfrac{1}%\n{1-x^{k}}.\n\\]\n\n\\end{theorem}\n\n\\begin{proof}\n[Proof of Theorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-0n}.]For each $n\\in\\mathbb{N}$, we\nhave%\n\\begin{align*}\n&  p_{0}\\left(  n\\right)  +p_{1}\\left(  n\\right)  +\\cdots+p_{m}\\left(\nn\\right) \\\\\n&  =\\left(  \\text{\\# of partitions of }n\\text{ whose largest part is }\\leq\nm\\right)  \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\left(  \\text{by Corollary\n\\ref{cor.pars.p0kn=dual}}\\right) \\\\\n&  =\\left(  \\text{\\# of partitions of }n\\text{ whose all parts are }\\leq\nm\\right) \\\\\n&  \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\left(\n\\begin{array}\n[c]{c}%\n\\text{because the condition \\textquotedblleft the largest part is }\\leq\nm\\text{\\textquotedblright\\ for a}\\\\\n\\text{partition is clearly equivalent to \\textquotedblleft all parts are }\\leq\nm\\text{\\textquotedblright}%\n\\end{array}\n\\right) \\\\\n&  =p_{\\operatorname*{parts}\\leq m}\\left(  n\\right)  ,\n\\end{align*}\nwhere $p_{\\operatorname*{parts}\\leq m}\\left(  n\\right)  $ is defined as in\nTheorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-parts-n}. Hence,%\n\\[\n\\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{N}}\\underbrace{\\left(  p_{0}\\left(  n\\right)  +p_{1}\\left(\nn\\right)  +\\cdots+p_{m}\\left(  n\\right)  \\right)  }_{=p_{\\operatorname*{parts}%\n\\leq m}\\left(  n\\right)  }x^{n}=\\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{N}}p_{\\operatorname*{parts}%\n\\leq m}\\left(  n\\right)  x^{n}=\\prod_{k=1}^{m}\\dfrac{1}{1-x^{k}}%\n\\]\n(by Theorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-parts-n}). This proves Theorem\n\\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-0n}.\n\\end{proof}\n\n\\subsubsection{Partition number vs. sums of divisors}\n\nWe shall now prove a curious combinatorial identity (due, I think, to Euler),\nillustrating the usefulness of generating functions and logarithmic\nderivatives. It connects the partition numbers $p\\left(  n\\right)  $ with a\nfurther sequence, which counts the positive divisors of a given positive integer:\n\n\\begin{theorem}\n\\label{thm.pars.sigma1}For any positive integer $n$, let $\\sigma\\left(\nn\\right)  $ denote the sum of all positive divisors of $n$. (For example,\n$\\sigma\\left(  6\\right)  =1+2+3+6=12$ and $\\sigma\\left(  7\\right)  =1+7=8$.)\n\nFor any $n\\in\\mathbb{N}$, we have%\n\\[\nnp\\left(  n\\right)  =\\sum_{k=1}^{n}\\sigma\\left(  k\\right)  p\\left(\nn-k\\right)  .\n\\]\n\n\\end{theorem}\n"
      }
    ],

        "text": "The set of partitions with largest part ≤ m equals the set of partitions\nwith all parts ≤ m (i.e., `restricted n (· ≤ m)`).\n\nThis is a key equivalence used in the proof of Theorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-0n}.",
        "policy": "source-facing Lean doc comment only; target Lean declaration name and statement remain withheld"
      },
      "target_comment_labels_or_parts": [],

      "instruction": "If the source snippet contains multiple labeled or numbered parts, formalize only the specific part/source span indicated by specific_source_labels, specific_labeled_parts, or target_declaration_source_comment. Do not conjoin all parts unless the record labels identify the whole multi-part result. The target_declaration_source_comment is source-facing prose only; it is not the hidden Lean target statement."

    "lean_prefix_context": "-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:108-108 (open)\nopen Nat Finset BigOperators PowerSeries\n\n-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:109-109 (open)\nopen scoped PowerSeries.WithPiTopology\n\n-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:376-376 (namespace)\nnamespace Nat.Partition\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:530-539\n/-- Elements of a multiset are bounded by the fold max. -/\nlemma fold_max_ge_of_mem (p : Multiset ℕ) (a : ℕ) (ha : a ∈ p) : a ≤ p.fold max 0 := by\n  induction p using Multiset.induction with\n  | empty => simp at ha\n  | cons b s ih =>\n    simp only [Multiset.fold_cons_left]\n    simp only [Multiset.mem_cons] at ha\n    rcases ha with rfl | ha\n    · exact le_max_left a (Multiset.fold max 0 s)\n    · exact le_trans (ih ha) (le_max_right b (Multiset.fold max 0 s))\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:685-688\n/-- The largest part of a partition (0 for the empty partition).\n    (Convention \\ref{conv.pars.largest-part-0}) -/\ndef largestPart {n : ℕ} (p : Partition n) : ℕ :=\n  p.parts.fold max 0\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:690-699\n/-- If all elements of a multiset are ≤ m, then fold max 0 is ≤ m. -/\nprivate lemma fold_max_le_of_all_le' (s : Multiset ℕ) (m : ℕ) (h : ∀ i ∈ s, i ≤ m) :\n    s.fold max 0 ≤ m := by\n  induction s using Multiset.induction with\n  | empty => simp\n  | cons a t ih =>\n    simp only [Multiset.fold_cons_left]\n    have ha : a ≤ m := h a (Multiset.mem_cons_self a t)\n    have ht : ∀ i ∈ t, i ≤ m := fun i hi => h i (Multiset.mem_cons_of_mem hi)\n    exact max_le ha (ih ht)\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/Partitions/Basics.lean:701-717\n/-- A partition has largest part ≤ m iff all its parts are ≤ m.\n    This is because the largest part is defined as the maximum of all parts.\n\n    This lemma is key for proving Theorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-0n}, which states that\n    the generating function for `p_0(n) + p_1(n) + ... + p_m(n)` equals `∏_{k=1}^m 1/(1-x^k)`.\n    The proof uses:\n    1. Corollary \\ref{cor.pars.p0kn=dual}: the sum equals the count of partitions with largest part ≤ m\n    2. This lemma: \"largest part ≤ m\" is equivalent to \"all parts ≤ m\"\n    3. Theorem \\ref{thm.pars.main-gf-parts-n}: the generating function for \"all parts ≤ m\" -/\ntheorem largestPart_le_iff_all_parts_le {n m : ℕ} (p : Partition n) :\n    p.largestPart ≤ m ↔ ∀ i ∈ p.parts, i ≤ m := by\n  unfold largestPart\n  constructor\n  · intro h i hi\n    exact le_trans (fold_max_ge_of_mem p.parts i hi) h\n  · intro h\n    exact fold_max_le_of_all_le' p.parts m h",
